topic: A technician performing a 'Three-Point Bend' test on a carbon fiber sample.
raw_generation:
{
  "script": "INT. LAB – DAY
A compact materials lab with a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) at the center. A carbon fiber coupon rests on two lower supports.

On-screen text: Three-Point Bend Test — Carbon Fiber Coupon

The TECHNICIAN enters frame wearing safety glasses, gloves, and a lab coat.

Tech: We'll begin by inspecting the coupon and verifying its dimensions before setup.

Cut to: Close-up of the coupon. The technician measures width b and thickness d with calipers and logs them.

Tech: The span length L is set according to the test method. The grip faces and anti-slip pads are in place.

Scene: Mounting the sample. The coupon rests on the two supports. The center loading nose is aligned above mid-span.

Tech: The sample is centered and clamped; align with reference marks.

Cut to: The machine prep screen showing zero and calibration.

Tech: We calibrate the load cell and deflection sensor; ensure zero is reached before loading begins.

Scene: Load application. The loading nose starts to move downward at a controlled rate.

Tech: Load is applied at a constant rate until the sample fails or a pre-set maximum deflection is reached. The P–δ curve is monitored in real time.

Cut to: Graph on monitor showing peak load Pmax and deflection at mid-span.

Tech: When failure occurs, we stop and record the peak load Pmax and the deflection.

Scene: Post-test. The specimen is removed; the fracture surface is inspected.

Tech: We compute flexural stress with sigma_f = (3 P L) / (2 b d^2) and log the results.

Scene: Results. The screen displays the computed values; the tech saves data and captions.

On-screen text: Results summary: Pmax, sigma_f, deflection at mid-span.

NARRATION: The three-point bend test evaluates the flexural strength of carbon fiber composites by applying a central load across a supported span. All steps—dimension checks, careful alignment, controlled loading, data capture, and post-test analysis—ensure accurate and repeatable results.",
  "narration": "Welcome to the materials lab. Today we perform a three-point bending test on a carbon fiber coupon to measure its flexural strength. We'll walk through setup, loading, and analysis, with safety as the top priority.

First, we inspect the coupon, check dimensions, and log b (width), d (thickness), and the chosen span L. The sample is mounted on the two supports of the universal testing machine, with the center loading nose positioned above mid-span.

We calibrate the machine, zero the load cell and deflection sensor, and ensure proper alignment and safe gripping.

The test begins at a controlled loading rate. The center nose applies load while the machine records load P and mid-span deflection δ. The resulting P–δ curve shows the material's response up to failure.

We stop at failure, record the peak load Pmax and corresponding deflection. Using beam theory, the flexural stress is sigma_f = (3 P L) / (2 b d^2).

Finally, we examine the fracture surface, capture data, and document the results. This test helps assess the bending strength of carbon fiber composites and compares different layups or resin systems."
}

parsed_generation.script:
INT. LAB – DAY
A compact materials lab with a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) at the center. A carbon fiber coupon rests on two lower supports.

On-screen text: Three-Point Bend Test — Carbon Fiber Coupon

The TECHNICIAN enters frame wearing safety glasses, gloves, and a lab coat.

Tech: We'll begin by inspecting the coupon and verifying its dimensions before setup.

Cut to: Close-up of the coupon. The technician measures width b and thickness d with calipers and logs them.

Tech: The span length L is set according to the test method. The grip faces and anti-slip pads are in place.

Scene: Mounting the sample. The coupon rests on the two supports. The center loading nose is aligned above mid-span.

Tech: The sample is centered and clamped; align with reference marks.

Cut to: The machine prep screen showing zero and calibration.

Tech: We calibrate the load cell and deflection sensor; ensure zero is reached before loading begins.

Scene: Load application. The loading nose starts to move downward at a controlled rate.

Tech: Load is applied at a constant rate until the sample fails or a pre-set maximum deflection is reached. The P–δ curve is monitored in real time.

Cut to: Graph on monitor showing peak load Pmax and deflection at mid-span.

Tech: When failure occurs, we stop and record the peak load Pmax and the deflection.

Scene: Post-test. The specimen is removed; the fracture surface is inspected.

Tech: We compute flexural stress with sigma_f = (3 P L) / (2 b d^2) and log the results.

Scene: Results. The screen displays the computed values; the tech saves data and captions.

On-screen text: Results summary: Pmax, sigma_f, deflection at mid-span.

NARRATION: The three-point bend test evaluates the flexural strength of carbon fiber composites by applying a central load across a supported span. All steps—dimension checks, careful alignment, controlled loading, data capture, and post-test analysis—ensure accurate and repeatable results.
parsed_generation.narration:
Welcome to the materials lab. Today we perform a three-point bending test on a carbon fiber coupon to measure its flexural strength. We'll walk through setup, loading, and analysis, with safety as the top priority.

First, we inspect the coupon, check dimensions, and log b (width), d (thickness), and the chosen span L. The sample is mounted on the two supports of the universal testing machine, with the center loading nose positioned above mid-span.

We calibrate the machine, zero the load cell and deflection sensor, and ensure proper alignment and safe gripping.

The test begins at a controlled loading rate. The center nose applies load while the machine records load P and mid-span deflection δ. The resulting P–δ curve shows the material's response up to failure.

We stop at failure, record the peak load Pmax and corresponding deflection. Using beam theory, the flexural stress is sigma_f = (3 P L) / (2 b d^2).

Finally, we examine the fracture surface, capture data, and document the results. This test helps assess the bending strength of carbon fiber composites and compares different layups or resin systems.

feedback:
The piece adheres to the topic and adds useful context, but its style is more verbose and script-like than the concise, action-first reference prompts. To improve, lean toward shorter, shot-focused prompts with minimal narration. Keep a single dominant subject per shot, and align terminology and formatting with the reference examples (clear variables, Pmax, delta, L, b, d).
Failed checks: example_alignment
strengths: Clear sequence of test steps and data points
Includes key variables (b, d, L) and the P–δ/strength calculation
fixes: Adopt the concise, action-first prompt style used in the references (limit to direct, camera-facing actions and shots)
Remove or minimize separate NARRATION blocks to maintain single-subject focus and reduce drift
